data.title: OFS Credit: I Would Only Consider Short-Term Preferreds
data.description:
Introduction

While I generally stay away from CLO equity investments, there are senior securities issued by CLO equity CEFs that are potentially interesting. Not in the least because these securities tend to have a minimum coverage ratio. OFS Credit Company (NASDAQ:OCCI) is a CET that predominantly invests in CLO Equity, which represented almost 92% of the total portfolio at the end of July. CLO Equity is great when everything goes right, but it’s the lowest quality tier when investing in CLOs, and a deteriorating credit quality could mean an increase in defaults, and that doesn’t bode well for CLO Equity investments.

That’s the main reason why I will focus on a more senior security in OFS Credit’s capital stack.

The NAV is decreasing fast

In order to determine the risks and the appeal of the senior securities in OCCI’s capital structure, I first need to have a look at the recent quarterly results of OFS Credit Company.

As you can see below, OCCI recorded an interest income of $11.9M in the third quarter, on which it generated a net investment income of $6.05M. As the company’s only financial liabilities are term preferred shares, it has to record the preferred dividends as interest expenses.

This means the preferred dividend coverage ratio in the third quarter was approximately 381%. And looking at the 9M results, the coverage ratio was approximately 395%. It is clear OFS Credit is in a good position to cover its preferred dividends.

The common units are worse off. Despite a $16.4M net investment income, OFS recorded in excess of $17M in realized and unrealized losses on its portfolio in the first nine months of the year. This, in combination with the dividend payments and the continuous increase of the total amount of shares outstanding, has resulted in a pretty severe erosion of the NAV/share, which dropped from $7.24 in July 2024 to just $6.13 by the end of July of this year. And in a subsequent update, OFS Credit mentioned it now expects its NAV to be between $5.50 and $5.60 per share at the end of September. This means it doesn’t look promising for the October NAV, which is when OFS’s financial year ends.

OCCIO: a six-month investment

As you can see below, OFS Credit has five series of preferred stock outstanding, of which three series are publicly traded. I’m mainly interested in (NASDAQ:OCCIO) as this series of term preferred stock benefits from the ‘seniority by maturity’ principle (it goes without saying that a substantial deterioration in the credit markets will put all preferreds at risk, even the near-maturity issues). The mandatory repayment date is just six months from now, and as OCCIO is trading at a discount to its call price of $25, it paves the way to generate a combination of (preferred) dividend income as well as a small capital gain.

The Series C term preferred stock was issued in 2021 and offers a 6.125% preferred dividend coupon. The preferred dividends are payable on a monthly basis, and this works out to $0.1276 per preferred share per month. This specific security has been callable since April 2023, but OFS Credit obviously didn’t call it, as the returns on CLO Equity were very high in the past few years and a 6.125% cost of equity is pretty low. But as it is a term preferred share, there also is a mandatory call date, which has been set at April 30, 2026.

This means that anyone buying the security now will receive six additional monthly dividends of $0.1276 per month while also receiving the $25 cash payment in April. Given the current share price of $24.92, this represents a total return of 3.4% in almost exactly six months. Every cent makes a difference, so I wouldn’t necessarily buy at $24.92 considering the stock is up 11 cents right now. But putting in an order at, for instance, $24.85 would result in a total return of approximately 3.7% in six months.

An additional interesting feature is the mandatory asset coverage ratio. CEFs have to provide a 200% asset coverage ratio on their preferred equity. At the end of the first semester of the current financial year, OCCI’s coverage ratio was approximately 238%, and this further improved to around 250% by the end of the third quarter (which ended in July). The mandatory coverage ratio is interesting, but it isn’t a guarantee: when the situation on the credit markets deteriorates fast, asset values may fall, and it may even be difficult to get a [fair] quote for CLO equity instruments.

Investment thesis

While I’m not interested in owning the common stock of OFS Credit, I think the more senior securities could be worth the additional attention. OCCIO is an interesting example of a short-term senior security, as the mandatory 200% asset coverage ratio provides an interesting protection mechanism.

I currently have no position in any of OFS Credit’s common or preferred stock, but I will keep an eye on the preferred stock issued by OCCI as long as the coverage ratio doesn’t rapidly deteriorate.

data.title: Nvidia: The Big 3 Issues Before Earnings
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) is set to report earnings on the 19th of November, and this may be the most important earnings yet for Nvidia and the AI industry.

A lot has been happening over the last few months that has shaken up the market, and it will be very interesting to see what Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has to say on the matter.

Nvidia faces three major issues as we approach the next earnings release, and these will be key points of discussion in the coming earnings call.

All in all, I see significant risk factors as we head into earnings, with a lot of room for Nvidia to disappoint.

With that said, an earnings overreaction could be a good place to add some exposure.

China: From Hero to Literal 0

This is something investors have been wondering for quite some time now.

China used to account for over 20% of Nvidia’s data center revenue, and though there has been hope in the last quarter that this could be salvaged with the right trade deals and products, it is now pretty much official that China is no longer an Nvidia fan.

As much was said by Jensen Huang in a recent interview with Citadel Securities.

We went from 95% market share to 0%, and so I can’t imagine any policymaker thinking that that’s a good idea, that whatever policy we implemented caused America to lose one of the largest markets in the world,

Source: TechRadar.

The good news is that, in theory, the current guidance was already not taking into account any potential revenue from China.

Current estimates have Nvidia at $54.66 billion for Q3. This is still a healthy 17% QoQ growth.

Assuming around 80-85 % of total revenue comes from Data Centers, then we’d be looking at $45 billion for the segment.

This would equate to QoQ growth of 9% which, to be fair, would be a re-acceleration compared to Q2. However, we must also consider that Q3 has usually been a seasonally strong quarter for Nvidia.

Is Competition A Problem?

Okay, the China thing may be priced in. But what about the recent increase in competitive alternatives to Nvidia?

As I highlighted in my most recent Nvidia article, companies like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are beginning to catch up in terms of performance.

The MI450 threatens to take market share from the Blackwell.

But of course, Nvidia is not going to sit idly by and let competitors take their market.

In this regard, we should expect to see a lot of talk about the next levels of the AI revolution.

Specifically, I’d like to update on NVLink Fusion, Nvidia’s recently launched custom silicone which enables Nvidia chips to be used with others in order to create custom AI data centers.

Beyond that, I/m sure there will be plenty of talk on the outlook for robotics and even quantum.

We, in fact, have the Nvidia GTC coming up in Washington D.C, which will give us a taste of what comes next.

Is AI A Bubble?

Lastly, I’m sure the AI bubble notion will be addressed at some point. Since Nvidia’s last earnings, there has been a mountain of deals which many have deemed to be circular and irresponsible.

Not only do I want to hear Jensen talk about the deals Nvidia has made, but also his outlook on companies like OpenAI (OPENAI) and the deals they have struck with companies like AMD and Oracle (ORCL).

As has been pointed out recently here on Seeking Alpha, Nvidia has a very robust and growing portfolio of AI ventures.

But this could become a double-edged sword if AI valuations peak.

Jensen should lay out a clear strategy of what the company is seeking to achieve with these investments.

Buying The Earnings Dip?

How does this all translate into earnings?

Here’s what I want to know:

  • Have Nvidia’s margins peaked given the increased competition?

  • Does the company have any hope of recovering China revenues?

  • Any new insights on Rubin’s successor, Feynman?

  • And with all the deals already in place to expand capacity, is there really room for more?

Hopefully Jensen will provide some clarity here.

Ultimately, though, I think there’s a pretty nice set-up for a selloff. Earnings so far have faced increased scrutiny from investors.

Netflix (NFLX) plummeted 10%, Tesla (TSLA) is down after earnings, and the broader stock market has been quite volatile in the last few weeks.

With all this uncertainty, valuations being still high, and technicals overstretched, a post earnings pullback would be very natural.

We have a bearish MACD crossover that just took place, and that generally leads to some weakness.

The 50 week EMA sits at $150, and I wouldn't be surprised to see Nvidia test somewhere close to those levels in coming weeks.

At that price, Nvidia would be more attractive, and I’d consider buying depending on what I see from the latest earnings.

Final Thoughts

Nvidia is the gold standard of AI, which makes their earnings so much more important. If Nvidia disappoints, then this could actually lead to a chain reaction that could be very bearish for AI stocks.

However, I’m still very excited to see what surprises Nvidia and Jensen have in store for us.

data.title: Markets Price In Another Fed Rate Cut Next Week, But Friday's Delayed Inflation Report Could Muddy The Waters
data.description:
By James Picerno

Market-based forecasts continue to expect the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates again next week at the Oct. 29 policy meeting. But getting from here to there requires going through tomorrow’s release of the delayed consumer inflation report for September.

Economists are looking for a mixed bag of numbers. The headline measure of the consumer price index (CPI) is on track to pick up to a 3.1% annual rate, the highest in nearly a year-and-a-half, according to Econoday.com’s survey. Core CPI, which strips out volatile food and energy prices and tends to be a more reliable measure of the trend, is projected to remain steady, also at 3.1%.

Assuming the estimates are correct, the CPI report will provide support for inflation hawks and doves, if only on the margins. The question is whether the update will strengthen or derail the case for another rate cut next week?

Policy is still moderately tight

TMC Research’s Fed funds model continues to indicate that monetary policy remains moderately tight. The effective Fed funds rate has been holding steady recently at roughly one percentage point above our neutral estimate. The gap leaves room for the Fed to cut while still leaving policy with a hawkish bias.

If there are concerns about upside inflation risk, it’s not showing up in the US 10-year Treasury yield, which continues to trade near its lowest level of the year.

Instead, the market and the Fed appear to remain more concerned about downside risks for the labor market. Last week Federal Reserve Chairman Powell said: “Inflation is above target and gently rising” while “the labor market is subject to pretty clear downside risks” in terms of hiring.

Labor market issues

Deciding if labor market risk remains a pressing concern, or not, has become challenging due to the government shutdown, which has delayed the September payrolls report.

Private-sector data can help fill in the gap, but alternative sources aren’t providing a clear picture of the labor market at the moment. The ADP Employment Report shows that private employers cut jobs for a second month in a row in September. Revelio Labs, a data analytics firm, paints a modestly brighter view and reports that hiring picked up last month, albeit from a low level.

Tomorrow’s CPI report could bring clarity, at least in terms of whether the Fed has a stronger or weaker case for more rate cuts. But if the consensus forecast is accurate, the inflation data will still leave room for debate.

This much is clear: the market will be forced to wait for the government to open for a clearer read on the numbers. Even if the shutdown ended today, tabulating and publishing new data will take time. The Fed, meanwhile, will be forced to make a new policy decision next Wednesday, well before the government starts updating economic figures.

An added complication for analyzing economic conditions: on top of the loss of data updates from various government agencies due to the shutdown, the payroll services firm ADP Research stopped providing its private-sector jobs data to the central bank following an Aug. 28 speech by Fed Governor Christopher Waller that referenced the numbers, according to The Wall Street Journal. The Fed’s already challenging job of assessing the economy seems to be getting tougher.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: Cipher Mining: A Solid Bitcoin Play, Though Not Without Risks
data.description:
Cipher Mining Inc. (NASDAQ:CIFR) is a Bitcoin mining and AI high-performance computing technology company. This is a dual, well-diversified business model that uses large-scale data centers for both Bitcoin mining operations and HPC hosting.

The CIFR stock price appreciated by roughly 236% over the past 12 months and significantly outperformed the benchmark. This resulted from Bitcoin appreciation and a pivot to a high-demand artificial intelligence landscape. The company has become less dependent on Bitcoin volatility, which the market has rewarded with a stock price increase.

Although I think that the decision to pivot to AI HPC has been the right one, I believe the CIFR stock price may have run ahead of its fundamentals. Despite the pivot to AI hosting, the company remains highly dependent on BTC, and with the current volatility, sentiment may change. Bitcoin seasonality could still be in play, but I think investors may shift to other legacy miners who have decided to pursue this high-demand landscape, too, but have not been rewarded with a premium yet.

This is why I think Cipher Mining is a Hold with a $20 price target over the next 12 months. I believe this company may be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who seek data center and Bitcoin exposure via the equity market, but I also think that investors could choose other alternatives available on the market, which could weigh on the stock price in the near term.

A Pivot To AI HPC Is A Game-Changer, But I Argue The Market Priced In Too High A Premium, Too Early

Cipher Mining is one of a few legacy Bitcoin miners that have decided to shift and diversify its business to AI HPC operations. However, CIFR and Iren Limited (NASDAQ:IREN) were the ones that aggressively shifted to this landscape, and the market has rewarded them with a significant premium. However, I think that investors may have priced in too much, slightly too early, and that could weigh on the stock price.

The chart above indicates that Cipher Mining has been the second-best performing legacy Bitcoin miner. Together with IREN, they have significantly outperformed CleanSpark and MARA Holdings, mainly due to an early shift to the AI HPC landscape. It does not necessarily mean that CIFR or IREN need to come down, but there appears to be a gap in valuations, which supports my thesis that the stock market may have run ahead of its fundamentals.

The chart below reveals the comparison of the following in terms of the total market capitalization. This appears to be interesting, highlighting that CIFR has roughly $7.11 billion in market cap, which is slightly below MARA Holdings, and about 30% above CLSK.

In terms of revenue (TTM), Cipher Mining appears to be the smallest Bitcoin and AI HPC play. CIFR generated roughly $159 million over the past 12 months, which is nearly 3x below CleanSpark's approximately $632 million and more than 4x lower than MARA Holdings of nearly $799 million. This indicates that there appears to be a gap in valuations, which does not necessarily result in CIFR stock decline, but may indicate that there are better alternatives in the market, which could weigh on the stock price.

Furthermore, MARA Holdings is the second-largest publicly listed Bitcoin treasury in the world with more than 53k coins. This equals about $5.7 billion in cash at the Bitcoin price of $108,000. CLSK has more than 13k coins, and CIFR holds roughly 1.5k Bitcoins in its treasury. This supports my thesis of a significant gap in valuations.

Cipher Mining produced 251 Bitcoin in September, while MARA produced 736 BTC and CLSK 629 BTC, highlighting a massive underperformance in terms of its mining operations and smaller scale versus its peers.

This is why I think that investors may shift to other alternatives in the market, which have not appreciated that significantly in their stock price, although following the same playbook, highlighting a pivot to the AI HPC landscape. There is also a possibility that if AI HPC demand continues, Cipher Mining may continue to appreciate in the stock price.

For instance, the high-performance computing market for AI was valued at about $112 billion in 2024, and is expected to triple in size over the next 4 years and reach roughly $360 billion by 2029. This highlights a 26.3% CAGR. If this materializes, then one could argue that CIFR remains cheap at the current valuations.

On the upside, on September 25, the company announced a long-term agreement with Fluidstack, underscoring nearly $3 billion in contracted revenue over the next 10 years. Google backstopped this and acquired nearly a 5.4% stake in the legacy Bitcoin miner. I think this deserves a higher premium, but based on my comparison, I believe it may be too elevated. This is why I think that in the near-term it could underperform its peers, as I argue that the market may have run ahead of its fundamentals.

CIFR Remains A Bitcoin Miner, And That Could Spark End-Of-Year Rally, If BTC Seasonality Plays Out

Although Cipher Mining pivoted to the artificial intelligence landscape, I think the market may continue to view it as a Bitcoin miner, and the stock price could be significantly impacted by the underlying volatility of the cryptocurrency.

On the upside, this could turn out to be favorable for CIFR, highlighting that the last quarter of the year historically has been a bullish period for the crypto environment in general, and BTC in particular.

The table above indicates that Bitcoin throughout the last 3 months of the year appreciated by 78% on average, and reflected nearly 48% median returns. So far, Bitcoin is down by 5% since the beginning of October, which may highlight that if favorable seasonality plays out, the cryptocurrency environment, including equities with crypto exposure, may experience significant upside.

I also think that the current crypto environment has additional tailwinds that fuel the bullish outlook and could spark an end-of-year rally. The current administration appears to be pro-crypto, which improves the investing landscape and encourages the adoption of digital assets. For instance, on July 18, President Trump signed the Genius Act, which establishes a framework for clearer stablecoin adoption. This is significantly important and also contributes heavily to the narrative of institutional adoption of the digital assets.

Furthermore, there are more publicly listed companies that are adopting the Bitcoin HODL strategy. Currently, Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) is leading with a massive amount of more than 640k BTC on its balance sheet. However, I think this substantially supports the bullish outlook for Bitcoin, as these companies may provide a floor price for the underlying by not selling their Bitcoin and continuing to accumulate coins as time passes by.

I think that although the cryptocurrency landscape currently experiences fairly negative sentiment supported by a roughly 5% Bitcoin decline, it remains a volatile industry, and with a significant and favorable shift, the seasonality could still be in play. If that materializes, I think Cipher Mining could continue appreciating in the stock price. However, if the crypto industry remains weak, this could meaningfully impact the stock price, and it could correct from these levels.

Cipher Mining Appears To Be Expensive, And I Am Not Sure Whether Fundamentals Justify It

CIFR currently trades at a forward P/E of 50.21, which highlights roughly a 2x premium versus the sector median of 25.49, and more than 2x overvaluation compared to the average company in the S&P500 at about 23.10x earnings multiple.

The chart above indicates that the current valuation also highlights a multi-year high, which supports a negative outlook for the company. This does not mean that CIFR can not trade at the current levels, but it signals that upside may be capped in the near term.

The company trades at forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales of 27.19 and 26.72, respectively, which underscores a 7x premium valuation compared to peers of 3.63 and 3.57. This implies that the market has priced in the future growth, and supports my thesis that investors may have run ahead of its fundamentals.

Cipher Mining appears to be a medium-leveraged business with total obligations of $189 million. The company has about $63 million in cash that partially offsets its debt, but leverage remains elevated. To highlight, this does not reflect its recent upsized private offering, which should translate to an estimate of $1.08 billion, which is expected to be used for data center expansion. Therefore, this underscores that CIFR may continue trading alongside other interest rate-sensitive names. The Fed anticipates reducing interest rates over the next two meetings by 0.25% each, and such a dovish monetary policy could be favorable for Cipher.

The legacy Bitcoin miner barely grew its revenue over the past 12 months, indicating a 0.11% increase and massive underperformance versus the sector median of 7.12%. Although the market anticipates double-digit growth, fueled by a pivot to AI hosting in the years ahead, this also supports my thesis that the market may have priced a significant premium slightly too early, limiting upside in the short term. Nevertheless, the company grew Capex by 530% over the past 12 months, and that highlights substantial focus on a high-demand AI landscape.

The company operates at below-market margins, and that is another argument for short-term consolidation. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 21.36% indicates more than 2x underperformance versus peers at 49.98%. The EBIT margin (TTM) of negative 69% underscores the disadvantage versus the sector median of 6.45% growth. I believe margins may improve with successful scale in the AI HPC landscape, but it also comes with execution risk. If AI demand weakens or does not translate into significant top and bottom-line growth, then current valuations may appear to be too inflated.

Wall Street forecasts a $0.36 in diluted EPS for FY2025, and $0.66 for FY2026. This would put CIFR at about 27x earnings multiple for the FY2026 figure. I think that the company should be able to achieve these results, especially if BTC seasonality plays out, or at least it continues trading at above the 100k mark over the next year. The recent agreement with Fluidstack appears to be promising and supports a bullish stance for the company.

I think that AI demand may continue further, but I also believe that the current forward P/E of more than 50x for FY2025 appears to be too elevated. If we take a $0.66 EPS estimate for FY2026, and apply a roughly 30x earnings multiple, we come in at my price target of nearly $20 over the next 12 months. This would signal 10% upside, but would likely underperform peers and reflect the benchmark performance.

Conclusion: Cipher Mining Appears Compelling, But I Think There May Be Better Alternatives In The Market

I think that Cipher Mining may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who favor a high-growth AI landscape of data centers and crypto exposure via the equity market. However, based on my analysis, I would argue there may be better alternatives among legacy Bitcoin miners, which have also pivoted to AI HPC, but have not experienced a massive surge in the stock price.

CIFR is also tied to execution risk, remains highly dependent on Bitcoin volatility, and future AI demand. At the current valuations, and after a massive triple-digit surge over the past 12 months, I think it may be extremely vulnerable if sentiment dramatically shifts. On the other hand, if AI-related demand continues, and BTC seasonality plays out, I believe CIFR could continue trading at an elevated multiple, especially if scaling in AI data centers plays out well.

This is why I think Cipher Mining is a Hold with a $20 price target over the next 12 months. This indicates 10% upside possibility, yet in line with the benchmark. 13 Wall Street analysts forecast a $19 price target over the next year, which presents a 5% upside opportunity. I feel more bullish, as I think that AI appears to be revolutionary, and data center services play a crucial role in the landscape. Time will tell, but after significant performance, I would not be surprised if the stock consolidates from here.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: The Prime Target In The Obesity Gold Rush
data.description:
Introduction

On my quest for the best pharmaceutical companies to snatch up while the sector has been facing a downturn, I came across Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:VKTX), which seems to be one of retail investors' favorites with its position as a high-risk, high-reward play. I first looked at Viking back in 2023, again as a potential takeover play, and was not expecting such a rollercoaster ride in its stock price since.

Currently, Viking Therapeutics is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company that focuses on developing therapies for metabolic and endocrine disorders, driven by the Blockbuster potential of VK2735, a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist. It has garnered a lot of M&A talks considering the high value of its drug pipeline.

Drug Pipeline

As the company does not currently have business segments, strictly speaking, it is hard to do my usual operation overview. However, that leaves us the chance to overview the company’s current pipeline.

VK2735 is currently the company’s most prominent program acting in the well known and covered glucagon-like peptide 1, or GLP-1, as well as the GIP receptor that has the same mechanism of action as Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Mounjaro and Zepbound. Viking is currently developing two different formulations, one injectable and another oral. The injectable VK2735 is currently in phase 3 clinical trials, with the preceding VENTURE trial demonstrating patients having up to 14.7% mean weight loss after 13 weeks of treatment. The Oral VK2735 is currently undergoing Phase 2 clinical trials, with its most recent study showing up to 12.2% mean weight loss after 13 weeks. This should directly put Viking as a direct key competitor to Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk (NVO) in the multibillion dollar race for anti-obesity drugs. VK2735 is currently why so many investors are bullish on the company, and why many believe that Viking could be a takeover target.

Viking is currently also developing VK2809, a drug designed to treat non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH). It just completed a Phase 2b trial that has met its primary endpoint and showed a significant reduction in liver fat content in patients. Viking developed another oral drug, VK0214, targeting a rare genetic neurological disorder, and is currently at earlier stages than the previous drugs, with a Phase 1b trial just being completed, showing that it was safe, well-tolerated, and reduced VLCFAs.

Current Dynamics

Viking Therapeutics just released its quarterly earnings, and though at the current stage of the company makes it difficult to assess, it posted a Q3 GAAP EPS loss of 81 cents, a miss of 11 cents due to the increase in R&D expenses, which now leaves the company with $715MM in cash and equivalents. Management has stated that this is sufficient for the time being, including the completion of the phase 3 trials for VK2735.

Most were looking toward the current trials, with Viking stating that the enrollment in the first phase 3 trials for its obesity pill VK2735 should be finished by December. The second trial should complete enrollment in Q1 2026 as well. For the Oral VK2735, the company will meet with the FDA in Q4 to discuss the next steps forward, mainly whether it can move towards a Phase 3 program. In all, I believe that the quarter was fairly good for shareholders, though yes, the increased burn rate is concerning; I believe that it is normal for such a late-stage company. The trials continue on their strong footing, and the company is on track to be commercially viable.

Outside of earnings, I want to touch on the M&A speculation that has been supporting the current stock valuation. VKTX initially jumped drastically back in early 2024 after Viking released its Phase 2 data for the injectable version of VK2735, which followed rampant market rumors that Pfizer (PFE) was in talks to acquire the company. The foundation of the rumors was fairly strong, based on Pfizer being desperate at the time and still now to deploy its vast cash resources to enter growth therapeutic areas such as weight-loss and obesity treatments. Acquiring Viking could have been a great and effective way to enter the market, and I still believe that it was the better choice over Metsera, that has fetched a total potential value of around $7.3B while “only” having its lead drug being in Phase 2. This lets us speculate on the valuation that Viking could fetch on the market, with the current valuation offering a discount.

I still believe that the M&A play is well on the table, with Viking being the last, and in my opinion, the most valuable independent late-stage company in the obesity space. If a giant like Merck (MRK) would want to enter the space, it would take 5 to 10 years and billions in R&D to create a GLP-1 drug and get it to Phase 3. Now, with each passing quarter, the company is essentially de-risking itself, becoming a better acquisition target.

The most cited companies that could take over Viking have been Merck, AstraZeneca (AZN), and Sanofi (SNY), which all have the capabilities to buy it as well as a strong presence in similar fields but lacking a GLP-1 asset. Mainly, Merck is in a race to replace Keytruda as its cash cow, as it is facing patent expiration, and Viking has the potential to become a blockbuster for the company. Pfizer could still buy VKTX, as Metsera assets are a long way from becoming commercially viable, and VKTX could provide a more direct way for commercial launch.

Valuation

Well, valuation-wise, there is not that much to say, unfortunately, as the company does not generate revenue or profits. So you won’t have my classic segments accompanied by my peer comparison graphs. However, based on the recent deal prices, premiums have been high, especially considering the Roche and Carmot deal that fetched a deal of up to $3.1B for a key drug entering only Phase 2 back then.

Now, investing in Viking Therapeutics would be highly speculative considering the lack of financial fundamentals for the company; that’s a certainty. However, I’m highly confident that the company’s current pipeline is one of the best value propositions in the healthcare sector, especially considering the current spotlight on weight-loss drugs.

Risks

As an investment would be highly speculative in Viking, I have to list more risks than usual. The main risk would be linked to clinical trial failure, with the main blow being a failure for VK2735, both injectable and oral, which would be devastating for the company’s current valuation.

Viking is also producing a drug that will fight in an intense competition environment, which is dominated by established giants like Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk, that have already highly effective drugs and have incredible manufacturing capabilities and strong market presence. Dethroning them, as well as the new entrants, could certainly be too tough a task for VKTX.

If Viking goes at it alone, it will face major hurdles in terms of manufacturing and commercial abilities, which will require another round of financing, with the biggest probability being share dilution, to the detriment of current shareholders.

Conclusion

I believe that with each passing minute, Viking is de-risking itself for potential takeovers, and though yes, investing in a potential M&A play is highly risky, it is rare to find such a pipeline gem at such a late stage in this particular industry still available on the market. For high-risk-tolerant investors, I rate Viking Therapeutics, Inc. stock a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FinVolution: A High-Risk, High-Reward Bet Providing An Asymmetric Upside Opportunity
data.description:
After covering stocks like QFIN (QFIN) and X Financial (XYF), I came to know about the cheap opportunities that the Chinese financial sector is offering. One such cheap opportunity, which is one of the top consumer finance players in China, is FinVolution (NYSE:FINV).

Similar to QFIN and XYF, FINV is also a good investment opportunity at cheap valuations and good growth prospects. Although there could be some kind of political or regulatory risk with respect to Chinese investments, I believe the reward potential outweighs the risk, which makes FINV a good investment.

FINV’s Business Model

FINV is a leading fintech platform offering consumer loans to young and underserved cohorts in the Chinese market and, more recently, in the Philippines and Indonesia. The company offers short-term loan products running from a tenure of one month to 24 months in the Chinese market, with an average tenure of eight months. In the international market, the tenure is much shorter, ranging from 1 month to 12 months, with an average tenure of 3 months. The company generates 80% of its revenue from the Chinese market and 20% from overseas markets.

FINV operates by matching borrowers with a diverse group of institutional funding partners. These partners include commercial banks, internet or digital banks, private banks, consumer finance companies, microloan companies, and trust management companies.

FINV bears credit risks for the majority of the loans funded by institutional funding partners, typically by providing quality assurance commitments (e.g., financing guarantees, sometimes through its own financing guarantee subsidiaries or third parties, or insurance policies).

Some undisclosed amount, but a minority of loan generation, is on a commission basis where FINV gets the commission for sourcing borrowers for leading partners without assuming credit risk.

Growth Opportunity And Capital Allocation

FINV has established a significant presence in the Chinese lending market, which is known for its rapid growth and large population. This favorable environment has been a tailwind for the company throughout its operational history. Even now, the Chinese consumer finance market is expected to grow at 7%.

In addition to domestic success, the company has recently started to pursue growth in the international market, particularly in developing Asian economies like Indonesia and the Philippines. The company has posted a revenue growth of ~74% CAGR from 2020 to 2024 in these developing markets, which shows the credit needs within these geographies that FINV is trying to address. Going forward, management intends to grow at ~30% CAGR in both of these geographies, which is quite substantial.

Given 80% revenue from the domestic market and 20% revenue from the international market, we get to an 11.6% segment-weighted projected growth rate based on 7% and 30% projected growth rates for the domestic market and the international market, respectively.

Segment Segment revenue contribution Projected growth rate
Domestic 80% 7%
International 20% 30%
Segment-weighted projected growth rate 11.6%

Source: Author, company filings.

This calculation does not even factor in the potential mix shift towards the higher-growth international markets. However, given that increased penetration over time will likely lead to a moderation in growth rates, an 11.6% overall growth figure appears to be a fair estimate.

While this growth rate is quite high, it lags far behind the ~20% ROE of the company. A lending business has the potential to grow at the pace of its ROE, given it can lend to quality borrowers at that pace. Hence, I believe this would leave FINV with enough cash flow to pursue growth in other geographies or distribute to shareholders.

As with exploring other geographies, the company is making efforts to enter into Pakistan's market. While this geography poses a considerable threat of political instability, it might not be a big market for FINV despite its large population. This move does support my earlier assumption of FINV’s geographic expansion. I would not be surprised if FINV makes an effort to enter into other Asian economies, particularly those with favorable relations with the Chinese government.

With respect to shareholder rewards, the company has been consistently giving out a dividend of 10% of the net profit and has cumulatively repurchased stocks worth $813 million, which is ~50% of the current market cap of $1.64 billion. More recently, in March 2025, the company enhanced its dividend policy to return 20-30% of net profit as a dividend, higher than the previous 10% return.

Risks

Tick Up In Delinquency Ratio

The overall trend of the delinquency ratio, leading (1-day) as well as lagging (90-day), has been declining over the last few quarters. The 1-day delinquency ratio has declined from the high of 5.7% in late 2023 to 4.7% now, and the 90-day ratio has also declined from the high of 2.65% in mid-2024 to 1.92% now.

While the overall trend looks quite positive, the most recent quarter's leading 1-day delinquency ratio showed a 10 bps increase over the preceding quarter. While the increase was quite nominal, when viewed in conjunction with QFIN’s increase in delinquency over the last two quarters and XYF’s increase over the last three quarters, there seems to be a trend emerging of deteriorating asset quality across the Chinese internet consumer finance companies, signaling a sector-wide asset quality concern.

FINV has been the best of the lot, with the 1-day ratio increasing just 10 bps in the most recent quarter. In comparison, QFIN witnessed a 40 bps increase in the 1-day delinquency ratio over the last two quarters, and XYF’s witnessed an increase of 14 bps for the 30 day delinquency ratio over the last three quarters, even after factoring in improvement in the recent quarter.

Regulatory Changes

Another key near-term risk for FINV is the regulatory changes in the domestic market, which aim to increase oversight on internet lending companies like FINV. New regulations, which came into effect in October, will lead to stricter supervision of companies like FINV through their lending partners. These regulations will partially restrict participation in very high-risk lending through these internet platforms and will mandate improved monitoring by lending partners of loans sourced by internet lending companies.

These changes should result in slower growth in the near term, as the lending standards will become stricter from now on. While management has reiterated their full-year revenue growth guidance, there is a possibility of a near-term disbursement growth shock resulting in revenue headwinds.

On the positive side, this regulation would decrease the systemic risk in the overall lending industry and decrease the delinquency ratio as lending standards would be stricter. Also, banks would favor large and established players like FINV over other smaller players as a sourcing partner, given their long history of successful sourcing.

Political Risk

The Chinese government does not allow foreign ownership of stocks in Chinese companies in certain sensitive sectors. To bypass this, many Chinese companies, including FINV, have adopted a variable interest entity (VIE) structure for their ADR. This structure gives ADR owners the right to the company’s profits but not an actual stake in the company.

In some extreme circumstances, the Chinese government may declare this structure illegal, leaving the owner of ADRs with no claim on the company's profits. While this is a possibility, I believe the probability of this happening is quite low. The spirit of the law restricting foreign ownership was to avoid foreign investors having a stake in Chinese companies, which could have potentially loosened the Chinese government's control over the private sector and, in a worst-case scenario, led to national security issues. However, the VIE structure allows Chinese companies to raise the much-needed growth capital as well as follow the spirit of the law by not offering an actual stake and control of the company.

Valuation

FINV is currently trading at a forward price-to-book ratio of 0.68x, which is in itself quite inexpensive. The company's Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) currently stands at 18%, with a 5-year average of 21%.

I believe valuing a lending business should be done by comparing the book value with the ROCE figure, as one tells about how the stock is valued compared to its book value and the other tells about how efficiently the company is using that book value to generate a return.

I also calculate the ratio of ROE over P/B, which I call "yield." For FINV, the yield is ~26.8% (18.8/0.7). Generally, a yield above 10% is considered good, and for risky lenders, a ratio above 15% is quite lucrative. Given FINV’s risk profile, I believe a yield above 15% is quite lucrative for the company. Even if we consider the political risk, I still believe FINV is quite attractively valued.

The undervaluation of FINV becomes even clearer when compared to its peers. A brief look at the yield ratio of FINV's peers shows substantial undervaluation of FINV as well as QFIN, which also has a business model similar to FINV and serves the Chinese market. Given FINV's lesser credit quality deterioration, I believe FINV is a better bet than QFIN in the near term.

Peers forward P/B ROE Yield
FINV 0.7 18.77% 26.81%
QFIN 0.96 31.11% 32.41%
PRG 1.93 34.34% 17.79%
LC 1.31 5.50% 4.20%
NAVI 0.48 1.24% 2.58%
EZPW 1.08 5.50% 5.09%
ECPG 1.01 -9.46% NA

Conclusion

Given the growth opportunity and cheap valuations, I believe FINV presents a compelling high-risk, high-reward opportunity. While there are inherent risks, the potential offered by FINV at its current valuation provides a significant upside. Therefore, I have a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Tesla: Priced For Perfection
data.description:
Electric vehicle giant Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) reported mixed results for the third fiscal quarter on Wednesday. The EV firm benefited from a strong delivery rebound in Q3 amid an expiry of federal tax credits in the September, which led to the company posting 12% year-over-year revenue growth. As of October 1, the U.S. government no longer offers a $7,500 tax credit for the purchase of an electric vehicle, which led consumers to pull forward EV purchases. This resulted in Tesla reporting its highest quarterly revenues ever.

Tesla’s gross margins also increased sequentially, and the firm’s free cash flow grew significantly faster than its revenue. However, investors will likely see a drop-off in deliveries and revenues in the fourth-quarter due to consumers front-loading their EV purchases in the last quarter. Since shares of Tesla are still very expensive in a historical context, I maintain my Sell rating following the firm’s Q3 earnings report.

Previous rating

I downgraded shares of Tesla to sell in September 2025 -- Why I Am Cashing Out Now -- mainly due to the company’s share price breaking out after the EV enterprise reported better-than-expected delivery results. In the third-quarter, Tesla benefited from a tax incentive-driven boost to deliveries, and the EV company benefited from strong growth in free cash flow. I believe this is still a great opportunity for investors to take profits and sell into the strength, as Tesla is most likely going to see weakening delivery growth going forward.

Tesla beat Q3 revenue estimates, missed on earnings

Tesla generated $0.50 per-share in normalized earnings in the third quarter, which missed the average Wall Street EPS prediction by $0.06 per-share. The EV enterprise also reported $28.1B in revenue, beating consensus expectations by a healthy $1.4B.

Tesla performed well in the third-quarter overall, mostly due to an expected surge in deliveries -- Tesla delivered a total of 497,099 EV units (+7% Y/Y) in Q3'25 -- leading the company to post record revenue of $28.1B (+12% Y/Y).

In terms of margins, Tesla had a very strong quarter as well: its gross margin, a key performance metric for electric vehicle companies, came in at 18.0%, showing a 0.8 PP margin gain compared to the previous quarter. It was also the second consecutive quarter in which the firm reported sequential margin gains.

As indicated in the introduction, the $7,500 tax credit for the purchase of an EV expired at the end of September, leading many buyers to buy EVs before then in order to quality for the subsidy. This led to a revenue and free cash flow windfall for the EV enterprise.

Tesla was, therefore, widely profitable in terms of free cash flow and realized serious growth on a Y/Y basis: in Q3 ’25, the company generated ~$4.0B in free cash flow, showing 46% year-over-year growth and a 3.3 PP FCF margin expansion compared to the year-earlier period. Tesla’s free cash flow also grew 3.8X faster than its top line in the third fiscal quarter.

Source: Author.

New EV product launches in China could cushion delivery drop-off in U.S.

While deliveries in North America are almost certain to drop off sequentially in the fourth-quarter following the expiration of U.S. tax credits, Tesla recently launched the Model Y L -- a six-seat, all-electric crossover SUV -- which started deliveries in September 2025. This SUV sold, according to Chinese sales lists, 8,221 units in the month of September.

The Model Y L has a starting price of 263,500 Chinese Yuan ($36,890) and is seeing strong order momentum as well which may at least somewhat cushion the blow of contracting of U.S. delivery volumes in Q4: last month, market research website CNEVPOST reported that the Model Y L was averaging a massive 10,000 orders per day, highlighting strong demand upside for Tesla's latest EV product.

China obviously holds a lot of promise for Tesla, as the country has one of the highest EV adoption rates in the world. Last month, Tesla sold 71,525 electric vehicles (+25% month-over-month) in China which was the second-highest figure this year, according to the China Passenger Car Association. Model Y L deliveries in China are, therefore, worth monitoring, and they could be a catalyst for stronger growth in this specific geography in Q4 and beyond.

Tesla is likely priced for perfection

Tesla’s shares are currently trading at a forward (FY 2026) revenue multiplier of 12.8X, which is about 62% above the firm’s longer term, 5-year average price-to-revenue ratio of 7.9X. Tesla is by far the most expensive EV play for investors and, in my opinion, likely overpriced.

Rivian Automotive (RIVN), a major U.S. rival in the electric vehicle market, is trading at a relatively low 2.24X forward price-to-revenue ratio… but Rivian also doesn’t have anything close to Tesla’s scale: the electric vehicle enterprise expects to deliver only up to 43,500 EVs this year.

Lucid Group (LCID), another domestic EV rival competing in the luxury EV segment, is trading at significantly lower price-to-revenue ratio of 2.26X as well, in large part because it is not yet profitable and has a much lower annual delivery volume. Tesla is globally established in the EV market and has material profitability which gives the company a competitive advantage in the event of an escalating price war in the electric vehicle industry.

In my previous work on Tesla, I calculated a fair value of $360/share, given the firm's historical valuation ratios. I believe this is still a reasonable price target considering that the EV company is headed for a drop-off in delivery volumes and revenue in the fourth-quarter. If shares dropped below $360, I would most likely consider buying back into the EV giant.

Risks with Tesla

The biggest risk for Tesla in the short term is a decline in deliveries and sales in the fourth-quarter as the EV company benefited from the timing of EV purchases in the third-quarter. Further, Cybertruck sales are not matching expectations and have broadly been a disappointment. In the third quarter, Tesla sold only 5,385 Cybertrucks, showing a 63% year-over-year decline rate. Further, tariffs on auto parts and growing pricing pressure in the EV market are risk factors for Tesla and other U.S. auto manufacturers and have the potential to lower profitability going forward.

Final thoughts

Tesla submitted a stronger-than-expected earnings sheet for the third quarter on Wednesday... which sent shares 4% lower in the after-hours market. Strong Q3 delivery results were already disclosed in October, so the market already expected a boost to the firm’s revenue and free cash flow, mainly because consumers tactically pulled buying decisions forward in order to qualify for expiring EV tax credits.

Tesla’s free cash flow grew 3.8X faster than its revenue, but a drop-off in both its FCF and revenues in the fourth-quarter must be expected given that a major incentive to buy EVs will no longer be available. While I like Tesla’s business model, scale and profitability, I am concerned about the firm’s high valuation based off of revenue, weaker-than-expected Cybertruck sales as well as weak short term delivery potential. Therefore, I confirm my Sell rating for the EV company following the third quarter earnings report.

data.title: VYM Is A Good Option Depending On Your Desired Strategy - Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Vanguard’s High Dividend Yield ETF (NYSEARCA:VYM) is a fund that seeks to track the performance of the FTSE High Dividend Yield index and provides investors with a way to earn consistent income and growth through large cap holdings. VYM is a hold for now - while they have a lower dividend yield than the ETF median, they do outperform their specific peer group and are priced very low.

See below for some key facts from the prospectus.

Their average 25 day volume is 1.4M, and their average 50 day volume is 1.3M. This is a highly liquid investment vehicle, based on those numbers from Vanguard.

Their top holdings are shown below.

VYM holds a number of leading firms across sectors and remains a strong way for investors to get exposure to growth and income.

This dividend strategy is popular for those combinations of benefits, and below we will review some other competitive funds that operate in this same realm.

VYM Competitors

This dividend ETF world is rife with competition, and using Seeking Alpha’s peer comparison tool we have selected a few major comparables of VYM. Please see the below chart that shows VYM versus these peers in a total return, over the past 1 year.

As you can see, starting from total returns, it is clear that not all dividend funds are equal. VYM actually outperformed all of its peers in the past year, and when examining this total return chart over a 5 year period, VYM continues to shine. See below.

Off performance of the fund alone, which includes dividend returns, it is clear that VYM is ahead of the pack.

When stacking expense ratios into the mix, as shown above, VYM clearly has the lowest expense ratio across its peer group at a lowly 0.06%. Purely from these two perspectives, it becomes evident that VYM is a great value play for investors - it has the best returns over time and it is priced at bargain basement levels.

When analyzing dividend funds, another angle that is important is stability - and in this case, we turn to the concentration and risk view from Seeking Alpha.

VYM appears to be near the top, along with DGRO and SDY, in terms of having the least assets in its top 10 names. This metric is important, because being too overweight in the top 10 holdings, no matter how strong of a company they might be, presents overconcentration risk where one or two major holdings can have an outsized impact on the portfolio overall. VYM has 27% of its assets in the top 10, and with over 569 holdings, comes out to being a pretty well diversified, lower risk fund overall in our view.

VYM Dividends

We look at the key stats related to VYM’s dividends to understand how they have changed in the past and compare them to the market.

We start by looking at the dividend scorecard from Seeking Alpha

We see right away that the picture does not look great for VYM. Compared to the median of all ETFs, they have a lower yield, lower (actually negative) growth in the last year and lower growth on a 5 and 10 year basis. Their only positive points are around the number of years of consecutive dividend growth.

We are not happy to see these numbers. As a “high dividend yield index”, we expect VYM to have just that - a high dividend. Instead, compared to the median of all ETFs (many of which do not have a dividend or are not dividend oriented), the fund lacks in both current yield and past growth.

When we look at VYM’s dividends compared to the peers we discussed, we see some similarities. VYM is in 2nd to last place in yield, 3 year growth and 5 year growth. We do see a 4 year average yield that is in the middle of the pack and their consecutive years of growth is the highest at 14 years. However, the number of consecutive years of growth and average 4 year yield do not make up for the low current yield and low historical growth.

Risks

Recession

Many of the top names in VYM including JP Morgan, Exxon, Wal-Mart, Johnson & Johnson, Home Depot, Procter & Gamble and Bank of America are either directly reliant on strong consumer demand or earnings from interest on things such as loans and mortgages. Both of these factors would be negatively impacted if a recession were to happen. It is hard to predict the exact risk of recession, some prominent economists are ringing the alarm bells for one to occur soon.

Value traps could exist within its holdings

Being that it is a high dividend fund, there is always the chance that some of VYM’s holdings may not be growing rapidly and are companies that could be hiding behind higher dividends while having a slowly shrinking or otherwise poor business. This could lead to overall underperformance of the fund if a larger number of these holdings fail to recover, but this risk should be low due to how diversified the fund is overall.

Conclusion

In conclusion, VYM is a well positioned high dividend fund that has outperformed its peers over the past 1 and 5 years, and has the lowest expense ratio compared to its peer group. VYM also is well diversified and is not overly concentrated in its top holdings. There are some potential issues with VYM as well, specifically their lower yield and growth rates compared to all ETFs or even the peers we explored earlier.

Overall, we see some strengths for VYM and some weaknesses. If you are looking for an ETF with high income and income growth, VYM might not be the option for you. However, if you are looking for an investment that balances between share appreciation and a solid dividend, VYM could be a great option. As such, we rate VYM as a hold.

data.title: Hammond Power Solutions: Big Downside Due To Declining Profitability
data.description:
Summary

Hammond Power Solutions Inc. (HPS.A) is a family-owned Canadian company that produces and sells dry-type transformers.

The stock has a peak-on-peak setup. This means that, due to favorable conditions post-COVID, the company is overearning while trading at a historically high valuation. I believe that these conditions are now changing and the market’s expectations for HPS.A margins and growth are too high.

Despite having limited exposure to markets tied to data centers and thus AI, the stock has outperformed its peer group, which has much larger exposure.

Company Overview

HPS.A is North America’s largest producer of dry-type transformers. A (dry-type) transformer is an electrical device that converts voltages without losing electrical energy (apart from minor losses due to heat) and does not use any liquid (i.e., oil) for cooling or insulation. This allows the electricity generated in power plants to be transmitted efficiently and transported to the end customer.

HPS.A mostly serves industrial end markets, like oil rigs and mines. Most of the revenue is generated in the US and Mexico (68%), and 27% is generated in Canada. The rest is generated in India via a Joint Venture.

In its market, HPS.A is the largest producer of dry-type transformers, with a market share of 23%, and the only publicly listed pure play. The rest of the market consists of smaller, local producers (26% of the total market) and a few large and globally diversified producers of electronic hardware like Schneider Electric, ABB, Siemens, and Hitachi (51% of market share).

Typically, this market has been cyclical, which depends on the demand from the end market as well as the production capacity. In recent history, HPS.A has benefited from both sides, demand and supply. Post-COVID, the company benefited from the economic recovery and an industry-wide undersupply and low production capacity for dry-type transformers. During this period, the lead times for dry-type transformers expanded massively. Usually, the lead times are two to eight weeks. In 2022, the lead times were ten to 16 weeks.

As a pure play, HPS.A was able to increase its price. Hammond increased its prices by more than 30%. Thus, the gross profit margin expanded and is currently 700 basis points higher than the last cycle’s peak.

As is often the case in cyclical industries, no situation lasts forever. According to people in the industry, the lead times have normalized to levels before COVID. Competition is increasing its capacity, and even HPS.A themselves.

Thus, I believe the current record-high margins are not sustainable for HPS.A. These products do not have any unique technological features that would enable HPS.A to continue to demand these high margins in the future.

The stock had a great run-up recently, which would make you think that it is an AI stock. In fact, the exposure to data centers, a massive beneficiary of AI, is very low. There is no way to provide an exact figure. However, the segment “Emerging Segment,” which includes EV charging infrastructure, semiconductors, and data centers, only accounts for 19% of the revenues. That is why I believe in HPS.A exposure to this theme is negligible. Especially because low-voltage transformers (which the company mostly produces) are not suitable for this end market. This is due to the data center's massive power consumption and low-voltage transformers that can transport energy without big efficiency losses and high risk of arcing, as well as short circuits. Yet, HPS.A outperformed its Peer Group, which is much better exposed to AI, by a wide margin.

Valuation

As discussed in the introduction, HPS.A is trading peak-on-peak. I have discussed the record-high margins, which are not sustainable in my opinion. HPS.A is currently trading at 20x P/E, compared to the previous cycle high of 17x earnings.

Historically, the company has traded at 11x earnings. In this respect, I see a high downside potential of 30% for the stock. My base scenario does not include a full return to historical margin profiles or valuation levels. Therefore, the downside in the worst-case scenario is over 60%.

Upcoming Earnings

The company will present its earnings for Q3 2025 on October 23rd, after market close. Last quarter, HPS.A grew revenues by 14% year-over-year, while gross profit margins were disappointing and declined 89 basis points. Thus, HPS.A missed EPS expectations by 39% (according to the Koyfin databank). Management attributed the decline in gross profit margins to rising input costs and investments in the manufacturing footprint. I believe this miss was responsible for the stock’s drop of more than 10% the following day. Going forward, I expect this trend to continue given the capacity expansion from the competition and HPS.A itself, as the new factories will be coming online in the coming months. Consensus estimates gross margins to be 30.55% for Q3. However, in the prior months, the margins declined by roughly 100 basis points, which is why I believe the consensus is too optimistic. Given operating leverage, this should trickle down to the EPS number, just like in Q2.

Risks

A risk to my short case is that the cycle could last longer than I expect, and thus HPS.A continues to over-earn compared to the past. Additionally, there could be a long-term tailwind for the business as the US power grid needs an upgrade. Maintaining current electricity consumption levels alone will require significant investment. However, given the current valuation, this best-case scenario is already reflected in the stock price, in my opinion.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tesla Stock Deserves To Fall Further
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:TSLA) announced record sales going into its most recent quarter, which we discussed in our last article. Those record sales were driven by expiring tax credits. Despite that, the company's Q3 earnings made it dip after hours, and it's not surprising to see why. As we'll see throughout this article, even the expiring tax credits put the company nowhere near where it needs to be to drive shareholder returns at its valuation.

Tesla Financial Summary

Tesla had strong financials for the quarter, but nowhere near sufficient to justify its valuation.

The company had $21.2 billion in total automotive revenues for the quarter, 12% YoY growth, but again with a massive ramp in volumes due to the expiration of tax credits. That YoY growth was driven by energy generation and storage along with services revenue with only 6% growth in automotive revenues for the quarter.

At the same time, on top of that revenue growth, the company saw a 1.85% decline in gross margins, a double-digit decline. The net result is the company's gross profits increased by only 1% or less than inflation. The company's operating margins also cut by nearly half resulting in a 9% decline in adjusted EBITDA.

So after that record quarter, the company saw an almost 40% decline in EPS, impacted by lower value regulatory credits. FCF remained reasonable for the company, in terms of growth, but TTM FCF is still just a hair above 1%.

Tesla Segment Breakdown

Looking at the company's segment breakdown we see other sources of concern.

Total production dropped 5% YoY despite a 7% increase in deliveries, as the company reported that its record deliveries were what we said above (a blip for the company).The company managed to cut its vehicle inventory YoY to a more reasonable level (10), but we expect it to sit at this lower production level.

Another major concern is the company's entire portfolio is still being driven by the Model 3/Y production. The company's Cybertruck seems to have completed its flop with -56% decline in production YoY and 30% decline in deliveries (including Model S and X). The company's entire business is the Model 3/Y which are lower margin products.

Tesla Capped Demand

Another sign of the company's capped demand is seen in its capacity.

The company's total vehicle production capacity across all of its lines is 2.3 million, or already 20% more than what it's selling on an annualized basis. The company has seen its market share in every major market peak if not decline, as competition has continued to line up and with no major new models on the horizon we don't see this changing.

The Model 3/Y have been in the market for a while, with refreshes, and the Cybertruck doesn't seem like demand will ever hit targets. The Tesla Semi and Cybercab are both years behind schedule, and given the Cybertruck results, we don't see them being needle movers.

Tesla Energy Segments

There is one bright spot in Tesla's portfolio, which is the company's energy generation and storage portfolio.

The company has continued to ramp its Powerwall and energy storage business, with 45 GWh in the most recent quarter. Tesla has a 15% global market share in energy storage, however, Chinese competitor Sungrow is catching up, with the gap shrinking to just 1%. Tesla lost its dominant position in Europe.

The market is still growing rapidly, and the company's energy and services and other gross profit has hit $16 billion annualized (though this includes services). Megapack prices have dropped substantially and they're at $250 million / GWh, or $12.5 billion annualized. The segment has ~30% margins, so $4 billion in annual profits from this segment.

it's respectable, but even at a sky-high P/E of 50x given its growth rate ~20% annualized for the last few quarters, it's worth $200 billion so 15% of the company's market cap. It's not a needle mover for the company's valuation.

Tesla's Optimus Can't Rescue

Tesla has switched from making ambitious claims about full self-driving ("FSD") to making substantial claims about the Optimus robot, perhaps because it's losing the self-driving race to Alphabet's (GOOG) Waymo and Amazon's (AMZN) Zoox.

The company "alluded" to the idea that the robot could be an excellent surgeon someday, which we'll file away in the long list of promises CEO Elon Musk makes that we don't think Tesla will see a penny from anytime soon. Anecdotal data indicates the the Optimus robot is on par with that of Figure AI, a privately held company valued at $39 billion.

Optimistically, that means Optimus is worth 3% of Tesla's market capitalization. Our concern is that Elon Musk, who is now almost a decade late on his FSD claims, has said that 80% of Tesla's revenue will come from Optimus. He hasn't highlighted a sustainable path or development timeline for that happening.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is a Tesla breakthrough. That is, if the company has some breakthrough with Optimus or self-driving that could propel the company to new heights. We'd argue a substantial part of that is already priced into the company's valuation at the present time.

Conclusion

Tesla declined almost 4% after-hours pushing its market cap to <$1.4 trillion. The company was punished despite a record quarter, as it appears that the boost was due to expiring tax credits instead of a sustainable shift in the company's business. The company appears to be hitting limits in demand as it settles into a steady state of ~1.5 million cars / year.

Tesla's Cybertruck volumes remain incredibly weak and there appears to be no sign of a resolution. The Roadster and Semi are some uncertain amount of time away, and at this point, even if the Cybercab launches without a safety driver, Tesla will be behind companies such as Waymo and Zoox. Going forward, we don't see a path to Tesla achieving its valuation, making it a poor investment.
